bytes.topic: Project Structure â€” MVC vs vertical slice, feature folders, layering conventions
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Project Structure â€” MVC Layers vs. Vertical Slices
bytes.body: Traditional ASP.NET Core projects mirror MVC's horizontal layers: `Controllers/`, `Services/`, `Repositories/`, `Models/`. This feels organized until a feature spans six folders and a simple change requires touching half the solution. Vertical slice architecture flips this: organize by feature, not layer. Each feature folder owns its controller (or minimal API endpoint group), command/query handlers (via MediatR), validators, and DTOs. Changes stay localized. A `Features/Articles/CreateArticle/` folder contains `CreateArticleCommand.cs`, `CreateArticleHandler.cs`, `CreateArticleValidator.cs`, and `CreateArticleResponse.cs` â€” everything needed, nothing else. In `Program.cs`, register handlers with `builder.Services.AddMediatR(cfg => cfg.RegisterServicesFromAssembly(Assembly.GetExecutingAssembly()))` and scan validators via FluentValidation's `AddValidatorsFromAssembly`. Shared infrastructure (DbContext, auth, logging) lives in `Infrastructure/` â€” not per-feature. The result: feature cohesion without sacrificing cross-cutting concerns. For greenfield ASP.NET Core 8 projects, vertical slices scale far better than horizontal layering as team size and feature count grow.

bytes.topic: Routing â€” defining routes, route parameters, grouping and prefixing
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Routing â€” Parameters, Groups, and Prefix Patterns
bytes.body:
ASP.NET Core's minimal API routing maps HTTP verbs directly to delegates via `MapGet`, `MapPost`, etc. Route parameters are declared with `{braces}` and bound automatically to handler parameters by name. Constraints like `{id:int}` or `{slug:regex(^[a-z-]+$)}` are applied inline â€” no attribute decoration needed.

Route groups (`MapGroup`) let you share a common prefix and middleware across related endpoints without repetition. Apply `.RequireAuthorization()`, `.WithTags()`, or rate limiting to the group once and every member inherits it. Groups nest cleanly: define a `/api/v1` outer group, then an `/articles` inner group â€” resulting paths compose automatically.

Controller-based routing still uses `[Route]` and `[ApiController]` attributes, but minimal APIs are preferred for new vertical slices. Use `WithName()` to assign route names for `TypedResults.Created()` link generation. Catch-all segments use `{**remainder}` syntax. Always prefer constrained parameters over manual parsing inside handlers â€” the framework rejects non-matching requests before your code runs.

bytes.topic: Dependency Injection â€” built-in DI containers vs manual wiring, testability impact
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core DI â€” Built-in Container vs Manual Wiring and What It Costs You in Tests
bytes.body:
ASP.NET Core's built-in `IServiceCollection` handles constructor injection, lifetime management (Transient/Scoped/Singleton), and scope validation at startup. For most apps, it's enough â€” and avoiding a third-party container like Autofac removes one moving part from your stack.

The real testability payoff comes from how you register: prefer `AddScoped<IOrderService, OrderService>()` over `AddScoped<OrderService>()`. Binding to the abstraction means xUnit tests swap in a `Mock<IOrderService>` with zero friction â€” no `HttpContext`, no `WebApplicationFactory` needed for unit tests.

Lifetime mismatches are a silent killer. Injecting a Scoped service into a Singleton causes `ValidateScopes` to throw at startup (enabled by default in Development). Production silently captures stale scope state. Always call `builder.Services.BuildServiceProvider(new ServiceProviderOptions { ValidateScopes = true, ValidateOnBuild = true })` in CI to catch these early.

For integration tests, `WebApplicationFactory<Program>` lets you call `.WithWebHostBuilder(b => b.ConfigureServices(s => s.AddSingleton<IEmailSender, FakeEmailSender>()))` to override any registration â€” no reflection hacks required.

bytes.topic: Middleware â€” request pipeline, global vs route-specific, ordering matters
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Middleware â€” Pipeline Order, Global vs Route-Specific
bytes.body:
ASP.NET Core's request pipeline is a chain of middleware components registered via `app.Use*()` in `Program.cs`. Order is deterministic and critical â€” each middleware calls `next()` to pass control downstream, then resumes on the way back. Authentication must precede Authorization; logging should wrap everything.

Global middleware (registered on `IApplicationBuilder`) runs on every request. For route-scoped behavior, use endpoint filters (`AddEndpointFilter`) or minimal API route groups with their own middleware chain via `MapGroup().Use*()`.

A common mistake: placing `app.UseAuthentication()` after `app.UseAuthorization()` silently breaks auth â€” claims are never populated when the authorization check runs. Similarly, `app.UseExceptionHandler()` must sit before all other middleware so it can catch downstream exceptions.

Use `app.UseWhen()` for conditional branching without short-circuiting the main pipeline, and `app.Map()` when you need a fully isolated sub-pipeline for a path prefix. Short-circuiting (not calling `next`) terminates the pipeline â€” intentional in auth failures, catastrophic if done accidentally in logging middleware.

bytes.topic: ORM & Database â€” query builders vs full ORMs, N+1 prevention, migrations
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core EF Core vs Dapper â€” Picking the Right Tool and Killing N+1
bytes.body:
ASP.NET Core gives you two dominant data access paths: EF Core (full ORM) and Dapper (micro-ORM/query builder). EF Core shines for complex domain models, migrations, and change tracking â€” use it with `DbContext` and model it with owned entities and value objects. Dapper wins for read-heavy endpoints where you want raw SQL performance with `IDbConnection.QueryAsync<T>`.

The N+1 problem bites hardest with EF Core's lazy loading. Disable it globally (`UseLazyLoadingProxies(false)`) and always eager-load with `.Include()`. For collections across large result sets, swap `.Include()` for `.AsSplitQuery()` to avoid the cartesian explosion that tanks performance on one-to-many joins.

For migrations, never run `dotnet ef database update` in production CI. Instead, generate idempotent scripts with `dotnet ef migrations script --idempotent` and apply them in your deployment pipeline. Use `MigrationBuilder.Sql()` for custom DDL that EF can't model. Keep migrations small and reversible â€” each one should have a valid `Down()` method.

bytes.topic: Authentication â€” JWT validation, session handling, auth middleware patterns
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core JWT Validation â€” Middleware, Claims, and Pitfalls
bytes.body:
ASP.NET Core's `AddJwtBearer` extension wires JWT validation into the authentication middleware pipeline. Configure it in `Program.cs` via `AddAuthentication().AddJwtBearer(...)`, setting `TokenValidationParameters` to control what's verified â€” issuer, audience, lifetime, and signing key. The middleware decodes the token, validates the signature against your `IssuerSigningKey`, and populates `HttpContext.User` with claims automatically before your endpoint logic runs.

Use `[Authorize]` on controllers or map it inline with `.RequireAuthorization()` on minimal API routes. For role-based or policy-based access, define policies in `AddAuthorization(options => options.AddPolicy(...))` and reference them by name.

A common mistake: forgetting to call `app.UseAuthentication()` before `app.UseAuthorization()`. Swapping the order silently breaks claim population â€” your endpoints see an unauthenticated user even with a valid token.

For sliding sessions, pair JWT with a refresh token stored in an `HttpOnly` cookie. On expiry, the client hits `/auth/refresh`, which validates the refresh token via a hashed lookup in your DB, then issues a new short-lived JWT. Never extend JWT lifetime as a substitute for proper refresh logic.

bytes.topic: Testing â€” unit vs integration, mocking HTTP, test database strategy
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Testing â€” Unit vs Integration, HttpClient Mocking, and Test DB Strategy
bytes.body: ASP.NET Core's WebApplicationFactory<TProgram> is the cornerstone of integration testing â€” it spins up a real in-memory host with your full middleware pipeline, DI container, and routing, letting you assert end-to-end HTTP behavior without deploying. Unit tests belong to handlers, validators, and pure service logic; keep them fast and dependency-free with Moq or NSubstitute. For outbound HTTP calls, inject IHttpClientFactory and swap in a mock MessageHandler via AddHttpClient().ConfigurePrimaryHttpMessageHandler() in your test â€” never mock HttpClient directly. For the database, prefer Respawn to reset state between tests against a real PostgreSQL instance rather than EF Core's InMemory provider, which silently ignores relational constraints and gives false confidence. Use a dedicated test connection string via environment variables or appsettings.Testing.json loaded via WebApplicationFactory.WithWebHostBuilder(). Mark integration tests with a custom [Trait("Category", "Integration")] and filter them out of your fast CI unit-test run with dotnet test --filter Category!=Integration.

bytes.topic: Input Validation â€” schema validation at the boundary, sanitization, error responses
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Input Validation â€” Schema Validation at the Boundary, Sanitization, and Error Responses
bytes.body: ASP.NET Core validates request bodies automatically when you annotate your DTOs with Data Annotations and call `builder.Services.AddControllers()` â€” but that's table stakes. For Minimal APIs, validation doesn't fire automatically; you must invoke it explicitly or wire a filter. Use `IValidator<T>` from FluentValidation for complex rules: it gives you composable, testable validators without polluting your domain models. Register with `builder.Services.AddValidatorsFromAssemblyContaining<Program>()` and inject into endpoint handlers or add a `ValidationFilter<T>` via `app.MapPost(...).AddEndpointFilter<ValidationFilter<CreateByteRequest>>()`. Sanitization is separate from validation â€” strip HTML at the boundary using `HtmlEncoder.Default.Encode()` or `AngleSharp` for rich content. Never trust `ModelState.IsValid` alone for security-sensitive fields; layer explicit sanitization on top. For error responses, conform to RFC 9457 (Problem Details): ASP.NET Core 8 emits `ProblemDetails` by default when `ApiBehaviorOptions.SuppressModelStateInvalidFilter` is false, giving clients a structured `errors` map with field-level messages. Extend `ValidationProblemDetails` for custom FluentValidation failure projections.

bytes.topic: Error Handling â€” global error handlers, consistent error response format
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Global Error Handling â€” Consistent ProblemDetails Responses
bytes.body:
ASP.NET Core 8 ships with built-in ProblemDetails support (RFC 9457). Wire it up once and every unhandled exception returns a structured JSON error â€” no per-controller try/catch needed.

Register `AddProblemDetails()` and `UseExceptionHandler()` in `Program.cs`. The exception handler middleware catches unhandled exceptions, maps them to `ProblemDetails`, and writes the response. For custom mappings, implement `IProblemDetailsWriter` or use `IExceptionHandler` â€” the cleaner option in .NET 8 â€” to intercept specific exception types before the fallback kicks in.

`IExceptionHandler` receives the `HttpContext` and the raw exception. Return `true` to short-circuit, `false` to fall through to the next handler. Chain multiple handlers for `ValidationException`, `NotFoundException`, `UnauthorizedAccessException` etc., each setting the appropriate HTTP status and `ProblemDetails.Type` URI.

Set `ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development` to expose `exception` details in the response; in Production they're stripped automatically. Pair with `IProblemDetailsService.WriteAsync()` for manual ProblemDetails responses inside minimal API endpoints or middleware.

bytes.topic: Security â€” CORS, rate limiting, security headers, injection prevention
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Security â€” CORS, Rate Limiting, Headers, and Injection in One Pass
bytes.body: Lock down your ASP.NET Core 8 API by layering four defenses. CORS: call `builder.Services.AddCors()` with a named policy, restrict `WithOrigins()` to known domains, and never use `AllowAnyOrigin()` with `AllowCredentials()` â€” it throws at startup. Rate limiting: use `AddRateLimiter()` with a fixed-window or token-bucket policy and apply `[EnableRateLimiting("policy-name")]` per endpoint or globally via `app.UseRateLimiter()`. Security headers: ASP.NET Core doesn't ship these by default â€” use `app.Use()` middleware to set `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, `X-Frame-Options: DENY`, and `Content-Security-Policy`. For injection prevention, never interpolate user input into raw SQL â€” use EF Core's parameterized LINQ or `FromSqlInterpolated()` (safe) over `FromSqlRaw()` with string concat (dangerous). Bind input through model binding with `[ApiController]` to get automatic `ModelState` validation, and annotate DTOs with `[MaxLength]` and `[RegularExpression]` to reject malformed data before it reaches your business logic.

bytes.topic: Performance â€” connection pooling, async handlers, response compression
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Performance â€” Connection Pooling, Async Handlers, and Response Compression
bytes.body: Three settings that meaningfully change throughput in ASP.NET Core apps often go untouched after scaffolding. First, Npgsql's connection pool defaults (`MaxPoolSize=100`) are usually too conservative for high-concurrency APIs â€” bump it via the connection string and configure `NpgsqlDataSourceBuilder` for named pools per tenant. Second, every Minimal API or controller action that touches I/O must be `async Task<IResult>` â€” synchronous handlers block Kestrel's thread pool under load; there's no automatic offloading. Third, `app.UseResponseCompression()` is opt-in. Register `AddResponseCompression` with Brotli first, gzip as fallback, and set `EnableForHttps = true` only after confirming your deployment terminates TLS at the app level, not a reverse proxy. Stack all three with `app.UseResponseCaching()` and `OutputCache` middleware for endpoints with stable payloads. Benchmark with `bombardier` or `k6` before and after â€” the async fix alone typically cuts p99 latency by 30â€“60% on DB-heavy routes.

bytes.topic: Serialization â€” request/response DTOs, camelCase vs snake_case, null handling
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Serialization â€” DTOs, camelCase, and Null Handling Done Right
bytes.body: ASP.NET Core's `System.Text.Json` serializer defaults to preserving property name casing, so a C# `UserId` property serializes as `UserId`, not `userId`. Fix this globally in `Program.cs` via `builder.Services.ConfigureHttpJsonOptions(o => o.SerializerOptions.PropertyNamingPolicy = JsonNamingPolicy.CamelCase)`. For snake_case (common in Python-facing APIs), use `JsonNamingPolicy.SnakeCaseLower` (.NET 8+). Always use dedicated request/response DTOs â€” never expose your EF Core entities directly. Decorate DTOs with `[JsonPropertyName]` when a single property needs a different name than the global policy. For null handling, set `DefaultIgnoreCondition = JsonIgnoreCondition.WhenWritingNull` to strip null fields from responses, reducing payload size. On input, `[Required]` + nullable reference types (`string?`) let the model binder distinguish missing from explicitly-null. Pair this with `[ApiController]` so missing required fields auto-return `400` without manual `ModelState` checks. For versioned APIs where field presence semantics matter, prefer `JsonIgnoreCondition.Never` and explicit null to avoid ambiguity between absent and null.

bytes.topic: Configuration â€” env vars, settings classes, secrets management
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Configuration â€” Env Vars, Typed Settings, and Secrets Done Right
bytes.body: ASP.NET Core's configuration system layers sources in priority order: appsettings.json â†’ appsettings.{Environment}.json â†’ environment variables â†’ command-line args. Environment variables override file-based config automatically â€” no extra code needed. Bind sections to strongly-typed classes using the Options pattern: register with `builder.Services.Configure<MySettings>(builder.Configuration.GetSection("MySettings"))`, then inject `IOptions<MySettings>` or `IOptionsSnapshot<MySettings>` (reloads on change). For secrets, never commit sensitive values. In development, use `dotnet user-secrets init` and `dotnet user-secrets set "MySettings:ApiKey" "abc123"` â€” stored in `~/.microsoft/usersecrets/`. In production, prefer Azure Key Vault via `builder.Configuration.AddAzureKeyVault(...)` or environment variables injected by your container orchestrator. Validate options at startup using `ValidateDataAnnotations()` and `ValidateOnStart()` to catch missing secrets before the app serves traffic â€” not at runtime when a request fails.

bytes.topic: API Design â€” REST conventions, versioning strategy, OpenAPI generation
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core API Versioning â€” REST Conventions, Route Strategy, and OpenAPI in One Setup
bytes.body: ASP.NET Core's `Asp.Versioning.Http` package gives you URL-segment, query-string, and header versioning with minimal config. The idiomatic approach pairs `MapGroup` with `AddApiVersioning` and `AddApiExplorer` to generate per-version OpenAPI documents via Swashbuckle or the built-in `Microsoft.AspNetCore.OpenApi` (introduced in .NET 9). Register versioning once in `Program.cs`, then decorate each `RouteGroupBuilder` with `.HasApiVersion(1, 0)`. For REST conventions: use plural nouns (`/bytes`, not `/getBytes`), map HTTP verbs semantically (`POST` creates, `PUT` full-replace, `PATCH` partial), and return `201 Created` with a `Location` header on resource creation via `TypedResults.Created`. Deprecate old versions by tagging `.HasDeprecatedApiVersion(1, 0)` â€” the `api-deprecated-version` response header signals consumers automatically. OpenAPI metadata flows through `WithOpenApi()` on each endpoint, letting you annotate summaries and examples inline without XML doc comments. Combine this with `[Produces]`/`[Consumes]` attributes on groups to enforce consistent content negotiation across all routes in a version.

bytes.topic: Logging â€” structured logs, correlation IDs, request logging middleware
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Logging â€” Structured Logs, Correlation IDs, and Request Middleware
bytes.body:
ASP.NET Core's built-in `ILogger<T>` supports structured logging out of the box. Instead of string interpolation, use message templates so log sinks (Serilog, OpenTelemetry) can index named properties: `_logger.LogInformation("Order {OrderId} placed by {UserId}", orderId, userId)`. This keeps logs queryable in tools like Seq or Grafana Loki.

For distributed tracing, inject a correlation ID on every request using middleware. Read `X-Correlation-ID` from the incoming header (or generate a new `Guid`), push it into `ILogger`'s ambient scope via `BeginScope`, and echo it back in the response header. Serilog's `LogContext.PushProperty` works identically.

Request logging middleware â€” either Serilog's `UseSerilogRequestLogging()` or a custom `IMiddleware` â€” gives you structured per-request entries (method, path, status, elapsed ms) without ASP.NET Core's noisy default host logs. Configure `appsettings.json` to suppress `Microsoft.AspNetCore` at Warning level while keeping your app namespace at Information, so production logs stay clean and actionable.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling â€” hot reload, code generation, scaffolding CLI
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Dev Tooling â€” Hot Reload, Scaffolding, and dotnet-aspnet-codegenerator
bytes.body:
ASP.NET Core's `dotnet watch` enables Hot Reload without restarting the process â€” file saves trigger a delta recompile and patch the running IL. For most controller/middleware edits, the app state persists. Rude edits (constructor changes, attribute additions) force a full restart, signaled in the console. Tune behavior in `launchSettings.json` via `"hotReloadEnabled": true` or pass `--no-hot-reload` when profiling.

For scaffolding, `dotnet-aspnet-codegenerator` generates controllers, Razor pages, and EF Core DbContexts from existing models. Install it once per machine: `dotnet tool install -g dotnet-aspnet-codegenerator`. Target a model and DbContext to emit a fully wired Minimal API or MVC controller with CRUD actions â€” saving 40+ lines of boilerplate per resource.

The `dotnet new` CLI ships first-class templates for Web API, gRPC, SignalR hubs, and Blazor. Custom templates via `dotnet new install <package>` let teams enforce internal conventions â€” authentication wiring, OpenTelemetry setup, health check endpoints â€” across every new service from day one.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Install scaffolding tool globally
dotnet tool install -g dotnet-aspnet-codegenerator

# Scaffold an API controller for a Product model using AppDbContext
dotnet aspnet-codegenerator controller \
  -name ProductsController \
  -async \
  -api \
  -m Product \
  -dc AppDbContext \
  -outDir Controllers

# Run with hot reload enabled (default in dotnet watch)
dotnet watch run --project ./MyApi/MyApi.csproj

bytes.topic: Monitoring â€” health check endpoints, metrics exposure, distributed tracing
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Monitoring â€” Health Checks, Metrics, and Distributed Tracing in One Pass
bytes.body: ASP.NET Core 8 ships three observability primitives out of the box. Health checks via `AddHealthChecks()` let you register named checks â€” database connectivity, external APIs, disk space â€” and expose them at `/health` with granular `HealthStatus` responses. Tag filters let k8s liveness and readiness probes hit separate subsets. For metrics, `AddOpenTelemetryMetrics()` wires into the built-in `System.Diagnostics.Metrics` API; the ASP.NET Core instrumentation library auto-emits request duration, active connections, and queue depth histograms â€” push them to Prometheus via `OpenTelemetry.Exporter.Prometheus.AspNetCore`. Distributed tracing uses `ActivitySource` and propagates W3C `traceparent` headers automatically through `HttpClient` and EF Core when you add `AddAspNetCoreInstrumentation()` and `AddEntityFrameworkCoreInstrumentation()`. All three pillars share one `IServiceCollection` registration pattern and export to the same OpenTelemetry collector, keeping your `Program.cs` composable. Pair with `dotnet-monitor` sidecar for on-demand dumps without redeployment.

bytes.topic: Background Jobs â€” task queues, scheduled jobs, worker process patterns
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core Background Jobs â€” BackgroundService, Channels, and Worker Patterns
bytes.body:
ASP.NET Core's `BackgroundService` (via `IHostedService`) is the backbone for long-running work. Inherit it, implement `ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken)`, and register with `builder.Services.AddHostedService<T>()`. For task queues, pair it with `System.Threading.Channels.Channel<T>` â€” a high-performance, built-in producer/consumer primitive. Your API enqueues work via `ChannelWriter<T>`, and your `BackgroundService` dequeues and processes via `ChannelReader<T>.ReadAllAsync()`. This avoids thread-unsafe `ConcurrentQueue` hacks and integrates naturally with `CancellationToken` propagation.

For scheduled jobs, ASP.NET Core has no built-in scheduler â€” use `PeriodicTimer` (introduced in .NET 6) inside `ExecuteAsync` instead of `Task.Delay` loops; it skips missed ticks cleanly. For production-grade scheduling with persistence and retries, Hangfire or Quartz.NET integrate cleanly via their ASP.NET Core packages.

For heavy workloads, extract the `BackgroundService` into a standalone Worker Service project (`dotnet new worker`) running as an Azure Container App job. It shares the same `IHostedService` model but decouples CPU-bound processing from your web API's request pipeline, preventing resource starvation.

bytes.topic: Framework-specific â€” Django batteries-included, FastAPI auto-docs, NestJS decorators, Phoenix LiveView
bytes.title: ASP.NET Core's Built-In Batteries: What You're Already Shipping Without a Package
bytes.body: Developers switching from Django, FastAPI, or NestJS often reach for third-party packages to replicate familiar features â€” but ASP.NET Core already ships most of them. FastAPI's auto-docs? Add `builder.Services.AddOpenApi()` and `app.MapOpenApi()` in .NET 9 â€” zero Swashbuckle required. NestJS-style decorators map directly to ASP.NET Core attributes: `[Authorize]`, `[RequiredScope]`, `[OutputCache]`, `[RateLimiting]` compose cleanly on Minimal API endpoints or controllers. Phoenix LiveView's server-driven reactivity is Blazor Server over SignalR â€” components re-render server-side, DOM diffs stream to the client. For Django's batteries-included feel, ASP.NET Core ships `IConfiguration` (layered config with env overrides), `IMemoryCache`, built-in health checks via `AddHealthChecks()`, and `IHttpClientFactory` with polly-backed resilience. The trap is reaching for NuGet when the `Microsoft.AspNetCore.*` namespace already has what you need. Run `dotnet add package` only after checking `WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args)` first.
